pages.full_text:
the Simplon railway tunnel, still one of the longest in the world.  Simon's return rail journey was interrupted when the guard became ill and all passengers were turfed off the train.  A curious episode occurred when a local woman shouted from across about seven tracks at one of my fellow passengers. She walked across two more lines and the man cursed others in response to her shouting.  She then crossed back again just before a fast express from Milan thundered through the station.  The man returned saying she was mad, but a few minutes later a carabiniero arrived and called the man over, much to the consternation of my fellow passengers who felt an affinity for the man.  After he had gone (via a subway) to the policeman they both reappeared and the policeman seemed to get evidence from another traveller — he ignored the woman and only spoke to a middle aged man!  Then the policeman and the accused departed and the rest of us settled down to wait for the next train.

Thursday
Simon cycled up to Vacciago to look at an exhibition of modern art but it turned out
pages.notes: No handwritten page number visible on this page. Text continues from a previous page (mid-sentence about the Simplon railway tunnel). The word 'carabiniero' may be 'carabiniere' — transcribed as written. 'Vacciago' is a village near Orta San Giulio, Italy; reading seems secure. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019.

pages.full_text:
to have changed its opening hours and was closed.  The swooping cycle ride down was exhilarating.

Friday
Eve's stomach ailment was receding after two days of starvation and a Diocalm pill so we set off quite early to drive north and then went up a side valley to the foot of Monte Rosa.  We parked in a concrete fenced part reserved for camping vans and took a skilift in two stages from Macugnaga to Belvedere from which we walked over a ridge to see the Monte Rose glacier.  This was disappointing as the glacier is completely covered in boulders and pebbles that it carries down the mountain like a carapace against the sun.  On closer examination, though, the view was fascinating as you could see that the glacier had been larger and one could make out the huge "U" shape that it had recently carved out of the landscape.

Eve was not well enough to go further walking so Simon left her to descend via the skilift while he crossed a side offshoot of the glacier to climb up the edge of the "U" to a chalet Zamboni.
pages.notes: Handwritten page number '19' visible in top-right corner. 'Monte Rose' appears in text — likely 'Monte Rosa' but transcribed as written. 'Belvedere' here refers to the ski/cable-car station above Macugnaga, not a separate town. 'chalet Zamboni' — Zamboni could be a proper name or hut name; transcribed as written. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019.
